forecasts.reasoning: The question asks if Kilmar Abrego Garcia will return to the United States before July 1, 2025. He was wrongfully deported in March 2025 to a prison in El Salvador. Despite court orders, including from the Supreme Court, to facilitate his return, both the Trump administration and Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele are vehemently opposed to it. As of April 30, 2025, Secretary of State Marco Rubio publicly refused to comply with the court orders, and President Bukele outright rejected diplomatic requests to return him, stating his administration does not release accused gang members. The Trump administration considers him a gang member. The U.S. executive branch is openly defying judicial orders to return him, and even if they wanted to, El Salvador currently holds jurisdiction over his physical custody and is refusing to release him. Given that only two months remain until the July 1, 2025 deadline and the absolute intransigence from both foreign and domestic executive branches, the probability of him returning in this timeline is extremely low. Even an unlikely scenario of contempt of court charges moving things along would take far longer than two months. The only way it could happen would be if an unforeseen political deal is brokered, which the latest news explicitly denies. I confidently put the probability near the floor allowed by the prompt.

There are multiple reports from early Q1 2025 (e.g., KED Global, Army Recognition, Janes, and Overt Defense) stating that Vietnam and South Korea's Hanwha Aerospace are finalizing or have already signed a $276M-$300M contract for 20-30 K9 Thunder self-propelled howitzers. An Army Recognition report from March 2025 even states the contract was 'signed' and 'unveiled in January 2025'. Because the negotiations have clearly progressed to the very final stages or have actually completed, the likelihood of a formal government announcement prior to the July 1, 2025 deadline is very high. The only remaining uncertainty is whether official government channels (e.g., DAPA or Vietnam's Ministry of National Defense) will formally announce the agreement by the stipulated date, or if it will remain technically unannounced/undisclosed due to geopolitical sensitivities or scheduling delays. Given the high degree of public reporting and progress, a resolution to YES is highly probable.

South Korea has seen immense protest activity (>10,000 participants) in early 2025 due to suspended President Yoon's impeachment trial, and the Carnegie Tracker was recently updated to include these. However, the resolution criteria strictly require the protest to have a *start date* AFTER the launch of this question (which is mid-January 2025, based on the ACLED background link). The Carnegie Tracker's methodology generally lumps ongoing political crises into single, continuous entries. It is highly likely the tracker assigns a December 2024 start date (when martial law was declared) to the entire wave of anti/pro-Yoon demonstrations, disqualifying them. Unless a distinctly new movement started after mid-January, the question will resolve No.

North Korea has been assessed as "prepared to carry out a nuclear test on short notice" since mid-2022, but has not conducted a test since September 2017. With only a two-month forecasting window remaining (May 1 to July 1, 2025), the base rate of a test occurring in this specific brief period is extremely low. While U.S. intelligence and South Korean authorities continue to issue warnings, similar predictions over the past three years (including before the 2024 U.S. elections) have failed to materialize. Although geopolitical tensions remain high and Kim Jong-un has called 2025 a "critical year," a nuclear test carries immense diplomatic risks, especially with China, and there are no signs pointing to an imminent test specifically scheduled for May or June. The probability over this short horizon is minimal.

The question asks whether the US will accuse Russia of invading any country other than Ukraine before July 1, 2025. With a knowledge cutoff of May 1, 2025, there are only two months left in the forecasting period. Through multiple targeted searches of US government sites, news domains, and think tanks, there are no ongoing new invasions, nor any indicators of imminent invasions by Russia into other countries (such as Moldova, Georgia, or the Baltics) in 2024 or early 2025. Russia is currently fully occupied with its massive conventional war in Ukraine, recently mobilizing additional troops to sustain efforts there, which structurally limits their capacity to initiate a new invasion. Occasional troop movements in Africa (e.g., Niger) occurred with the consent of those governments and would not meet the resolution criteria of "without consent from the government". The base rate of a new invasion within a short two-month window is already exceedingly low (< 1%), and the lack of any precursors or intelligence warnings of an impending attack further depresses the probability. Therefore, the chance of the US accusing Russia of a new invasion before July 1, 2025, is assessed at the floor probability of 2%.

As of late April 2025, Libya has not yet ratified the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) Agreement. Libya has been listed continuously over the last few years as one of a small handful of countries that signed the agreement but have yet to deposit their instrument of ratification. Currently, Libya is deeply divided politically between rival governments which makes the ratification of international treaties by its parliament (the House of Representatives) highly difficult and generally not a priority. There are also no current news reports in either English or Arabic indicating any ongoing momentum or drafting in the Libyan legislature regarding the AfCFTA. With the resolution date (July 1, 2025) merely two months away from the knowledge cutoff, the timeline is exceedingly tight for any unexpected political breakthrough to result in swift ratification. Therefore, the probability of Libya ratifying before July 1, 2025 is extremely low.
